title: Constructors 🏗️
description: How to properly build a brand new object.

slides.title: The Tedious Setup 🥱
slides.content:
Right now, building an object sucks.
`Dog myDog = new Dog();`
`myDog.name = "Rex";`
`myDog.age = 3;`
`myDog.breed = "Husky";`

Imagine doing that 4 lines of code for 100 dogs. We need a way to set all this data UP FRONT the moment the object is born.

slides.title: The Constructor Method 🏗️
slides.content:
A **Constructor** is a special method that runs EXACTLY ONCE automatically the moment you use the `new` keyword.

Its job is to build ("construct") the object and set up its initial variables.

It has two weird rules:
1. It must have the EXACT SAME NAME as the Class.
2. It has NO return type (not even `void`).

slides.title: Constructing a Dog 🐕
slides.content: Look at the `Dog(...)` method inside the class. That's the Constructor. We feed it parameters, and it uses them to initialize the Object's State.

quizData.question: Which of these is a valid Constructor definition for a class named `Player`?
quizData.explanation: Constructors must perfectly match the Class name, cannot have a return type like `void`, and typically aren't `static`.

quizData.question: If you define a constructor `Dog(String n, int a)`, can you still create a dog simply using `new Dog();`?

options.text: Yes, Java automatically fills in blank values.
No, if you define a custom constructor, you MUST provide the arguments it demands.

quizData.explanation: If you build a custom constructor, Java disables the default empty one. Anyone building a Dog is now FORCED to provide a name and age. This prevents bugs!
